source_text: “It’s a poor boy from the free-school, sir,” replied Mr. Bumble, “who has been nearly murdered—all but murdered, sir,—by young Twist.”
raw_output: “It’s a poor boy from the free-school, sir,” replied Mr. Bumble, “who has been all but murdered—nearly murdered, sir—by young Twist.”
rewrite_text: “It’s a poor boy from the free-school, sir,” replied Mr. Bumble, “who has been all but murdered—nearly murdered, sir—by young Twist.”

source_text: “Here’s fresh meat for your axe, Kreis,” he said; “a rose-white youth with the ardor of a lover for Herbert Spencer. Make a Haeckelite of him—if you can.”
raw_output: “Here is fresh meat for your axe, Kreis,” said he; “a rose-white youth, with the ardor of a lover for Herbert Spencer. Make a Haeckelite of him—if you can.”
rewrite_text: “Here is fresh meat for your axe, Kreis,” said he; “a rose-white youth, with the ardor of a lover for Herbert Spencer. Make a Haeckelite of him—if you can.”

source_text: “No, miss,” returned Jerry, “it shall not be named to you. Second: them poor things well out o’ this, and never no more will I interfere with Mrs. Cruncher’s flopping, never no more!”
raw_output: “No, miss,” returned Jerry, “it shall not be named to you. Second: them poor things well out o’ this, and never no more will I meddle with Mrs. Cruncher’s flopping, never no more!”
rewrite_text: “No, miss,” returned Jerry, “it shall not be named to you. Second: them poor things well out o’ this, and never no more will I meddle with Mrs. Cruncher’s flopping, never no more!”

source_text: On a coarse-grained level, we can categorize existing AMR parsing approaches into two main classes: Two-stage parsing (Flanigan et al., 2014; Lyu and Titov, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a ) uses a pipeline design for concept identification and relation prediction, where the concept decisions precede all relation decisions; One-stage parsing constructs a parse graph incrementally. For more fine-grained analysis, those one-stage parsing methods can be further categorized into three types: Transitionbased parsing (Wang et al., 2016; Damonte et al., 2017; Ballesteros and Al-Onaizan, 2017; Peng et al., 2017; Guo and Lu, 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Naseem et al., 2019) processes a sentence from left-to-right and constructs the graph incrementally by alternately inserting a new node or building a new edge. Seq2seq-based parsing (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Konstas et al., 2017; van Noord and Bos, 2017; Peng et al., 2018) views parsing as sequence-to-sequence transduction by some linearization of the AMR graph. The concept and relation prediction are then treated equally with a shared vocabulary. The third class is graph-based parsing (Cai and Lam, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b) , where at each time step, a new node along with its connections to existing nodes are jointly decided, either in order (Cai and Lam, 2019) or in parallel (Zhang et al., 2019b) . raw_output: At a coarse-grained level, existing AMR parsing methods can be divided into two principal categories. Two-stage parsing (Flanigan et al., 2014; Lyu and Titov, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a) adopts a pipeline framework for concept identification and relation prediction, in which concept decisions are made before any relation decisions. By contrast, one-stage parsing builds the parse graph incrementally. For a more fine-grained view, one-stage methods can be further split into three types: Transition-based parsing (Wang et al., 2016; Damonte et al., 2017; Ballesteros and Al-Onaizan, 2017; Peng et al., 2017; Guo and Lu, 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Naseem et al., 2019) reads a sentence from left to right and incrementally constructs the graph by alternating between adding a new node and creating a new edge. Seq2seq-based parsing (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Konstas et al., 2017; van Noord and Bos, 2017; Peng et al., 2018) treats parsing as sequence-to-sequence transduction through a linearization of the AMR graph. Concept and relation prediction are then handled uniformly with a shared vocabulary. The third category is graph-based parsing (Cai and Lam, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b), in which, at each time step, a new node and its links to existing nodes are decided jointly, either sequentially (Cai and Lam, 2019) or in parallel (Zhang et al., 2019b). Two-stage parsing (Flanigan et al., 2014; Lyu and Titov, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a) adopts a pipeline framework for concept identification and relation prediction, in which concept decisions are made before any relation decisions. By contrast, one-stage parsing builds the parse graph incrementally. For a more fine-grained view, one-stage methods can be further split into three types: Transition-based parsing (Wang et al., 2016; Damonte et al., 2017; Ballesteros and Al-Onaizan, 2017; Peng et al., 2017; Guo and Lu, 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Naseem et al., 2019) reads a sentence from left to right and incrementally constructs the graph by alternating between adding a new node and creating a new edge. Seq2seq-based parsing (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Konstas et al., 2017; van Noord and Bos, 2017; Peng et al., 2018) treats parsing as sequence-to-sequence transduction through a linearization of the AMR graph. Concept and relation prediction are then handled uniformly with a shared vocabulary. The third category is graph-based parsing (Cai and Lam, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b), in which, at each time step, a new node and its links to existing nodes are decided jointly, either sequentially (Cai and Lam, 2019) or in parallel (Zhang et al., 2019b). source_text: As suggested by the definition of data Shapley, computing data Shapley value requires an exponentially large number of computations to enumerate O(2 N train ) possible subsets and train the model M on each subset, which is intractable. Inspired by (Jia et al., 2019a) , CMADE tackles this issue by reducing the deep model M to a knearest neighbors (KNN) model and then apply the closed-form solution of shapley value on KNN. Using the feature extractor φ trained in Stage 1 and Stage 2, we fix φ and map all dialogs in the training data
raw_output: As implied by the definition of data Shapley, calculating the data Shapley value entails an exponentially large number of computations, since one must enumerate O(2 N train ) possible subsets and train model M on each subset, which is computationally infeasible. Motivated by Jia et al. (2019a), CMADE addresses this problem by replacing the deep model M with a k-nearest neighbors (KNN) model and then applying the closed-form Shapley value solution for KNN. With the feature extractor φ learned in Stage 1 and Stage 2, we keep φ fixed and project all dialogues in the training data
rewrite_text: As implied by the definition of data Shapley, calculating the data Shapley value entails an exponentially large number of computations, since one must enumerate O(2 N train ) possible subsets and train model M on each subset, which is computationally infeasible. Motivated by Jia et al. (2019a), CMADE addresses this problem by replacing the deep model M with a k-nearest neighbors (KNN) model and then applying the closed-form Shapley value solution for KNN. With the feature extractor φ learned in Stage 1 and Stage 2, we keep φ fixed and project all dialogues in the training data

source_text: Approach. Our approach ( Figure 2 ) uses pretrained neural models to interpret the explanations E in the context of a given input x. Formally, we define an interpreter I as any function that takes an input x and explanation e j and produces a feature vector in R d . In our ExpBERT implementation, we choose I to capture whether the explanation e j is entailed by the input x. Concretely, we use BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) finetuned on MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018) : we feed wordpiece-tokenized versions of the explanation e j (hypothesis) and the instance x (premise), separated by a [SEP] token, to BERT. Following standard practice, we use the vector at the [CLS] token to represent the entire input as a 768-dimensional feature vector: 1These vectors, one for each of the n explanations, are concatenated to form the explanation representation
raw_output: Approach. Our method (Figure 2) employs pretrained neural models to interpret the explanations E in the context of a given input x. Formally, we define an interpreter I as any function that accepts an input x and an explanation e_j and outputs a feature vector in R d. In our ExpBERT implementation, we instantiate I to determine whether the explanation e_j is entailed by the input x. Specifically, we apply BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) fine-tuned on MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018): we provide BERT with wordpiece-tokenized versions of the explanation e_j (as the hypothesis) and the instance x (as the premise), separated by a [SEP] token. Following standard practice, we use the vector at the [CLS] token to encode the full input as a 768-dimensional feature vector: 1These vectors, one for each of the n explanations, are concatenated to form the explanation representation
